answer:
**Cynewulf's *Juliana* (epilogue):** On the "Great Day," the Trinity—Father, Son, and Holy Spirit—will sit in majesty as Judge of deeds, assigning eternal rewards or punishments to every person according to their works.[^1]

**Viṣṇu Smṛti** (late chapters, e.g., ch. 97 on yoga/eschatology):** One who meditates properly on the imperishable *puruṣa* (the 25th principle, Viṣṇu) attains him and becomes liberated (*muktim āpnoti*), transcending rebirth.[^2]

Cynewulf's final reckoning is far more visible: a dramatic, universal "Great Day" with the Trinity publicly judging all humanity's deeds at once, evoking doomsday imagery throughout the poem (e.g., hell's king, soul's journey, rewards in glory). Viṣṇu Smṛti's is abstract and individual—quiet yogic insight yielding mokṣa, with no collective event or vivid judgment scene (late chapters emphasize meditation over hells/rebirth, which appear earlier, e.g., ch. 44).[^3]

Viṣṇu Smṛti gives the wicked vastly more time to repent: sins lead to temporary hells and animal rebirths, but one can pursue yoga/liberation across countless lives via karma. *Juliana* implies a single life's deeds seal fate until the final Judgment (no reincarnation), though repentance is urged before death.[^4]

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Juliana*, ch. 1 [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-1f8ce8).

[^2]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, ch. 97 [paragraph](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/97/translation/#p-92883f).

[^3]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, ch. 44 [chapter](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/44/translation/).

[^4]: Cf. *Juliana*, ch. 1 [paragraphs](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-e769dc) (soul judged by life's deeds post-separation).
